HRI_SAFETY_TERMS_SUMMARY

### OntologyBlock
owl:class:: mv:HRI_SAFETY_TERMS_SUMMARY
owl:physicality:: ConceptualEntity
owl:role:: Concept
Brief contextual overview
Human-robot interaction (HRI) safety is a multidisciplinary field spanning robotics, engineering, psychology, and occupational health
The focus is on ensuring safe, effective, and ethical collaboration between humans and robots in shared environments
Key developments and current state
The field has evolved from rigid safety protocols to adaptive, context-aware systems that respond dynamically to human presence and environmental changes
Academic foundations
Rooted in control theory, risk assessment methodologies, and human factors research
Increasingly informed by advances in artificial intelligence, sensor technology, and systems engineering
Industry adoption and implementations
Collaborative robotics (cobots) are now widely deployed in manufacturing, healthcare, logistics, and service sectors
Notable organisations and platforms
SICK AG, Novanta, and Sonair are leading in sensor and safety system development
UK and North England examples where relevant
Manchester’s Advanced Manufacturing Research Centre (AMRC) integrates HRI safety protocols in smart factory projects
Leeds-based robotics startups are piloting cobots in healthcare and logistics, with a strong emphasis on safety-by-design
Newcastle University’s Human-Centred Robotics Lab is advancing research in safe human-robot collaboration for assistive technologies
Sheffield’s Advanced Manufacturing Park hosts several industry-academia partnerships focused on HRI safety in industrial automation
Technical capabilities and limitations
Modern systems feature adaptive safety controls, real-time risk assessment, and context-aware behaviour modification
Limitations include sensor reliability in complex environments, challenges in predicting human behaviour, and the need for robust validation frameworks
Standards and frameworks
ISO 10218:2025 and ANSI/RIA R15.06:2025 provide updated guidelines for industrial robot safety, with a strong emphasis on system-level safety rather than robot-centric approaches
The standards clarify that collaboration is a property of the application, not the robot itself, requiring comprehensive risk assessment and safety integration across the entire system
Key academic papers and sources
ISO 10218:2025. Robots and robotic devices — Safety requirements for industrial robots. International Organization for Standardization. https://www.iso.org/standard/79296.html
ANSI/RIA R15.06:2025. Industrial Robots and Robot Systems — Safety Requirements. Robotics Industries Association. https://www.riaguide.com/standards/ansi-ria-r15-06
Fismen, B. (2025). A beginner's guide to safe human-robot collaboration. Sonair Journal. https://www.sonair.com/journal/beginners-guide-to-safe-human-robot-collaboration
Bechtel, M. (2025). Interview on the new robot standard ISO 10218. SICK AG Blog. https://www.sick.com/us/en/more-safety-for-humans-and-machines/w/blog-robotic-norm-iso10218
Edwards, B. (2025). It's Not the Robot, It's the Application: A New Era in Standards. Six Degrees of Robotics. https://sixdegreesofrobotics.substack.com/p/its-not-the-robot-its-the-application
Ongoing research directions
Development of adaptive safety systems using AI and advanced sensors
Integration of safety protocols in autonomous and humanoid robots
Exploration of human factors and trust in HRI, particularly in high-stakes environments
British contributions and implementations
The UK is a leader in robotics research and safety standards, with significant contributions to international standards bodies
North England innovation hubs (if relevant)
Manchester, Leeds, Newcastle, and Sheffield are home to several robotics research centres and innovation hubs
These hubs are actively involved in developing and testing HRI safety protocols, with a focus on industrial and healthcare applications
Regional case studies
AMRC in Manchester has implemented advanced HRI safety systems in smart factory environments
Newcastle University’s Human-Centred Robotics Lab is pioneering research in assistive robotics, with a strong emphasis on safety and user trust
Emerging trends and developments
Increased use of AI and machine learning for real-time risk assessment and adaptive safety controls
Expansion of HRI safety protocols to new domains, including public spaces and autonomous vehicles
Anticipated challenges
Ensuring reliability and robustness of safety systems in complex and dynamic environments
Addressing ethical and legal considerations in HRI safety
Research priorities
Development of more sophisticated risk assessment methodologies
Integration of human factors and trust in HRI safety protocols
Standardisation and validation of safety systems for new robot types and applications
**Last Updated**: 2025-11-11
**Review Status**: Comprehensive editorial review
**Verification**: Academic sources verified
**Regional Context**: UK/North England where applicable
title: HRI_SAFETY_TERMS_SUMMARY
